『[[古事記]]』では崇神天皇の章に、[[大彦命|大毘古命]]を[[越国|高志道]]（こしのみち）に、[[建沼河別命]]を「東の方十二道」に、日子坐王（ひこいますのみこ、日本書紀では[[彦坐王]]）を[[丹波国|旦波国]]にそれぞれ遣わしたとの記載がある。また、崇神天皇の3代前である[[孝霊天皇]]の章に、大吉備津日子命（吉備津彦命）と若日子建吉備津日子命（日本書紀では[[稚武彦命]]）の2柱が「[[播磨国|針間]]を道の口として[[吉備国]]を言向け和し」た、とある。四道将軍の名称は記載されていない。
また、『[[常陸国風土記]]』では[[武渟川別]]が、『[[丹後国風土記]]』では[[丹波道主命]]の父である[[彦坐王]]が記述されている。
四道将軍の説話は単なる神話ではなく、[[豊城入彦命]]の派遣や[[ヤマトタケル]]伝説などとも関連する王族による国家平定説話の一部であり、初期[[ヤマト王権]]による支配権が地方へ伸展する様子を示唆しているとする見解がある。事実その平定ルートは、[[4世紀]]の[[前方後円墳]]の伝播地域とほぼ重なっている。
== 神話での記述 == {{出典の明記|date=2020年4月|section=1}} [[大彦命]]は、[[孝元天皇]]の第1皇子で、母は皇后の[[欝色謎命]]（うつしこめのみこと）。[[開化天皇]]の同母兄で、娘は[[崇神天皇]]皇后の御間城姫命（みまきひめのみこと）、[[垂仁天皇]]の外祖父に当たる。[[舟津神社]]（[[福井県]][[鯖江市]]<!--舟津町1丁目3-53-->）、[[敢國神社|敢国神社]]（[[三重県]][[伊賀市]]）、[[伊佐須美神社]]（[[福島県]][[会津美里町]]）、[[古四王神社]]（[[秋田県]][[秋田市]]）等に祀られている。[[敢國神社|敢国神社]]の社伝によると同神社より北東1.5Kmの所に位置する[[御墓山古墳]]は大彦命の御陵とする。[[敢國神社|敢国神社]]の鎮座する[[三重県]][[伊賀市]]は[[大彦命]]の子孫である[[阿倍氏|阿閉臣]](阿敢臣)の発祥の地とされる伝承も残されている。
[[武渟川別]]は、大彦命の子。[[阿倍氏|阿倍朝臣]]等の祖と伝えられる。[[津神社]]（[[岐阜県]][[岐阜市]]<!--曽我屋字屋敷1631-->）、[[健田須賀神社]]（[[茨城県]][[結城市]]<!--結城195-->）等に祀られている。
また『古事記』によれば、[[北陸道]]を平定した大彦命と、[[東海道]]を平定した[[武渟川別|建沼河別命]]が合流した場所が[[会津]]であるとされている。（会津の地名由来説話）。このときの両者の行軍経路を[[阿賀野川]]（大彦命）と[[鬼怒川]]（武渟川別）と推察する見解が哲学者の[[中路正恒]]から出されている。
[[吉備津彦命|吉備津彦]]は、[[孝霊天皇]]の皇子で、母は[[倭国香媛]]（やまとのくにかひめ）。[[倭迹迹日百襲姫命]]は姉である。別名は'''彦五十狭芹彦'''（ひこいさせりひこ）。[[吉備国]]を平定したために吉備津彦を名乗ったと考えられている。[[桃太郎]]のモデルの一つであったとも言われている。[[吉備津神社]]、[[吉備津彦神社]]（[[岡山県]][[岡山市]]）、[[田村神社 (高松市)|田村神社]]（[[香川県]][[高松市]]）等に祀られている。
[[丹波道主命]]は、古事記によると[[開化天皇]]の子の[[彦坐王]]の子。なお、古事記では[[彦坐王]]が丹波に派遣されたとある。母は[[息長水依比売娘]]（おきながのみずよりひめ）。娘は[[垂仁天皇]]皇后の[[日葉酢媛命|日葉酢姫]]（ひばすひめ）。[[景行天皇]]の外祖父に当たる。神谷神社（[[京都府]][[京丹後市]]<!--久美浜町大字久美浜字小谷-->）等に祀られている。
== 考古学上の成果 == [[大彦命]]に関しては、[[埼玉県]]の[[稲荷山古墳 (行田市)|稲荷山古墳]]から発掘された[[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣|金錯銘鉄剣]]に見える乎獲居臣（ヲワケの臣）の上祖・'''[[意冨比垝]]'''（オホビコ、オホヒコ）と同一人である可能性が高いとする見解が有力である<ref>[[川口勝康]]（首都大学東京教授）は次のように解説する。「稲荷山古墳出土の鉄剣銘文中の乎視居臣 (おわけのおみ) なる人物の系譜にみえる上祖の意富比魁は、オホヒコとよまれ、記紀の大彦命にあたる可能性が高い（平凡社『世界大百科事典』）」。また、[[岸俊男]]（京都大学名誉教授）は次のように解説する。「ヲワケを東国国造の系譜に属する者と考える説と、上祖オホヒコを記紀に阿倍臣や膳臣 (かしわでのおみ) の始祖としてみえる孝元天皇の皇子大彦命とし、あるいは杖刀人は阿倍臣に従属する丈部(はせつかべ) であるとみて、ヲワケを中央豪族の一員と考える説に大きく見解が分かれている（平凡社『世界大百科事典』）」。[[安本美典]]は、『[[本朝皇胤紹運録]]』によると「大彦命」の孫は「豐韓別命」であり、鉄剣銘文の「意富比垝（オホヒコ）」の孫「弖已加利獲居（テヨカリワケ）」と読み方が似ているとする（[http://yamatai.cside.com/katudou/kiroku246.htm 倭王武と雄略天皇 稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣銘文]）。また、豐韓別命は[[武渟川別]]の子とされるが、鉄剣銘文では弖已加利獲居は[[多加利足尼]]の子である。</ref>。このことから、四道将軍が創作された神話ではなく、実際に伝承された祖先伝説を元に構成されたことを示している。
[[崇神天皇]]陵に比定されている[[行燈山古墳]]（墳丘全長約242m、後円部直径約158m、後円部高さ約23m）と[[吉備津彦命]]の[[陵墓]]参考地（大吉備津彦命墓）である[[中山茶臼山古墳]]（墳丘全長約120m、後円部直径約80m、後円部高さ約12m）はサイズがほぼ2対1の相似形であることが指摘されている<ref>安本美典 『巨大古墳の主がわかった!』 JICC出版局 1991年。行燈山古墳は[[文久の修陵]]の際に修復が行われており、被葬者については異説もあること、また中山茶臼山古墳は[[吉備の中山]]の自然の地形を利用して築造されていることに注意。</ref>。
== 備考 == * [[前田晴人]]は四道将軍の神話自体は史実とは認めないものの、ここに描かれた「'''四道'''」を[[五畿七道]]成立以前のヤマト王権の地域区分であったとし、四道将軍はその成立を説明するために創作された説話であるとする説を唱えている。 * [[前田晴人]]の2014年時点での見解は<!-- 参考後述書 pp.158 - 171. -->、四道という語は[[6世紀]]の[[国造]]制再編による影響によって導入された中華思想的な概念とする<ref>『ここまでわかった! 「古代」謎の四世紀』 [[新人物文庫]] 2014年 ISBN 978-4-04-600400-0 p.171.</ref>。論拠として、[[5世紀]]の[[倭の五王]]の一人である武（[[雄略天皇]]）が出した[[上表文]]には、「東西南北」ではなく、「東西」を平定したといった内容が記され、東西を基軸とした国家統治を基本として、本来、こちらが弥生期から続く伝統的な観念であり<ref>同『ここまでわかった! 「古代」謎の四世紀』 p.171.</ref>、『[[古事記]]』においても、「西道」（[[吉備国]]）の記述がなく、これにともなって「四道」の語もない<ref>同『ここまでわかった! 「古代」謎の四世紀』 p.161.</ref>ことから、4世紀時点に四道の語はなかったとする。
== 脚注 == <references />

This title referred to four individuals: {{ill|Ōbiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}},..."
revision.text:
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | Image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. This title referred to four individuals: {{ill|Ōbiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}}, {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}}, and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where {{ill|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津日子命}} and Wakakibitsuhiko no Mikoto subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early {{ill|Yamato|ja|大和政権}} authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳}}.
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and {{ill|Utsushikome no Mikoto|ja|欝色謎命}}, was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, {{ill|Mimaki Hime no Mikoto|ja|御間城姫命}}, became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * {{ill|Ōbiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} * {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}} * {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} * {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}

{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | Image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where {{ill|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津日子命}} and Wakakibitsuhiko no Mikoto subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early {{ill|Yamato|ja|大和政権}} authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳}}.
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and {{ill|Utsushikome no Mikoto|ja|欝色謎命}}, was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, {{ill|Mimaki Hime no Mikoto|ja|御間城姫命}}, became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | Image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * [[Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}

=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * [[Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}
[[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Titles of Japanese generals]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Figures in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Male members of the imperial family]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[ja:四道将軍]]

{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | Image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * [[Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|四道]] '''四道将軍'''（しどうしょうぐん、古訓：よつのみちのいくさのきみ）は、『[[日本書紀]]』に登場する[[皇族]]（[[王族]]）の[[将軍]]で、[[大彦命]]（おおびこのみこと）、[[武渟川別|武渟川別命]]（たけぬなかわわけのみこと）、[[吉備津彦命]]（きびつひこのみこと）、[[丹波道主命]]（たんばみちぬしのみこと）の4人を指す。<ref name=syoki>「以大彦命遣北陸。武渟川別遣東海。吉備津彦遣西道。丹波道主命遣丹波。因以詔之曰。若有不受教者。乃挙兵伐之。既而共授印綬為将軍。」[http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html 日本書紀（朝日新聞社本）]</ref>
== 概要 == {{出典の明記|date=2020年4月|section=1}} 『[[日本書紀]]』によると、[[崇神天皇]]10年にそれぞれ、[[北陸]]、[[東海]]、[[西道]]、[[丹波国|丹波]]に派遣された。なお、この時期の「[[丹波国]]」は、後の[[令制国]]のうち[[丹波国]]、[[丹後国]]、[[但馬国]]を指す。 教えを受けない者があれば兵を挙げて伐つようにと将軍の[[印綬]]を授けられ<ref name=syoki/>、翌崇神天皇11年、地方の敵を帰順させて凱旋したとされている<ref>「四道将軍以平戎夷之状奏焉。」[http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html 日本書紀（朝日新聞社本）]</ref>。なお、崇神天皇は[[3世紀]]から[[4世紀]]の人物とされている<ref>『日本の歴史 第1巻 神話から歴史へ』[[中央公論新社#著名な刊行物|中央公論社]],1964 文庫新版,2005,ISBN 978-4122045477</ref><ref>『古事記』は崇神天皇の没年を[[戊寅]]年と記し、[[258年]]または[[318年]]とする説が有力である。</ref>。
『[[古事記]]』では崇神天皇の章に、[[大彦命|大毘古命]]を[[越国|高志道]]（こしのみち）に、[[建沼河別命]]を「東の方十二道」に、日子坐王（ひこいますのみこ、日本書紀では[[彦坐王]]）を[[丹波国|旦波国]]にそれぞれ遣わしたとの記載がある。また、崇神天皇の3代前である[[孝霊天皇]]の章に、大吉備津日子命（吉備津彦命）と若日子建吉備津日子命（日本書紀では[[稚武彦命]]）の2柱が「[[播磨国|針間]]を道の口として[[吉備国]]を言向け和し」た、とある。四道将軍の名称は記載されていない。
また、『[[常陸国風土記]]』では[[武渟川別]]が、『[[丹後国風土記]]』では[[丹波道主命]]の父である[[彦坐王]]が記述されている。
四道将軍の説話は単なる神話ではなく、[[豊城入彦命]]の派遣や[[ヤマトタケル]]伝説などとも関連する王族による国家平定説話の一部であり、初期[[ヤマト王権]]による支配権が地方へ伸展する様子を示唆しているとする見解がある。事実その平定ルートは、[[4世紀]]の[[前方後円墳]]の伝播地域とほぼ重なっている。
== 神話での記述 == {{出典の明記|date=2020年4月|section=1}} [[大彦命]]は、[[孝元天皇]]の第1皇子で、母は皇后の[[欝色謎命]]（うつしこめのみこと）。[[開化天皇]]の同母兄で、娘は[[崇神天皇]]皇后の御間城姫命（みまきひめのみこと）、[[垂仁天皇]]の外祖父に当たる。[[舟津神社]]（[[福井県]][[鯖江市]]<!--舟津町1丁目3-53-->）、[[敢國神社|敢国神社]]（[[三重県]][[伊賀市]]）、[[伊佐須美神社]]（[[福島県]][[会津美里町]]）、[[古四王神社]]（[[秋田県]][[秋田市]]）等に祀られている。[[敢國神社|敢国神社]]の社伝によると同神社より北東1.5Kmの所に位置する[[御墓山古墳]]は大彦命の御陵とする。[[敢國神社|敢国神社]]の鎮座する[[三重県]][[伊賀市]]は[[大彦命]]の子孫である[[阿倍氏|阿閉臣]](阿敢臣)の発祥の地とされる伝承も残されている。
[[武渟川別]]は、大彦命の子。[[阿倍氏|阿倍朝臣]]等の祖と伝えられる。[[津神社]]（[[岐阜県]][[岐阜市]]<!--曽我屋字屋敷1631-->）、[[健田須賀神社]]（[[茨城県]][[結城市]]<!--結城195-->）等に祀られている。
また『古事記』によれば、[[北陸道]]を平定した大彦命と、[[東海道]]を平定した[[武渟川別|建沼河別命]]が合流した場所が[[会津]]であるとされている。（会津の地名由来説話）。このときの両者の行軍経路を[[阿賀野川]]（大彦命）と[[鬼怒川]]（武渟川別）と推察する見解が哲学者の[[中路正恒]]から出されている。
[[吉備津彦命|吉備津彦]]は、[[孝霊天皇]]の皇子で、母は[[倭国香媛]]（やまとのくにかひめ）。[[倭迹迹日百襲姫命]]は姉である。別名は'''彦五十狭芹彦'''（ひこいさせりひこ）。[[吉備国]]を平定したために吉備津彦を名乗ったと考えられている。[[桃太郎]]のモデルの一つであったとも言われている。[[吉備津神社]]、[[吉備津彦神社]]（[[岡山県]][[岡山市]]）、[[田村神社 (高松市)|田村神社]]（[[香川県]][[高松市]]）等に祀られている。
[[丹波道主命]]は、古事記によると[[開化天皇]]の子の[[彦坐王]]の子。なお、古事記では[[彦坐王]]が丹波に派遣されたとある。母は[[息長水依比売娘]]（おきながのみずよりひめ）。娘は[[垂仁天皇]]皇后の[[日葉酢媛命|日葉酢姫]]（ひばすひめ）。[[景行天皇]]の外祖父に当たる。神谷神社（[[京都府]][[京丹後市]]<!--久美浜町大字久美浜字小谷-->）等に祀られている。
== 考古学上の成果 == [[大彦命]]に関しては、[[埼玉県]]の[[稲荷山古墳 (行田市)|稲荷山古墳]]から発掘された[[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣|金錯銘鉄剣]]に見える乎獲居臣（ヲワケの臣）の上祖・'''[[意冨比垝]]'''（オホビコ、オホヒコ）と同一人である可能性が高いとする見解が有力である<ref>[[川口勝康]]（首都大学東京教授）は次のように解説する。「稲荷山古墳出土の鉄剣銘文中の乎視居臣 (おわけのおみ) なる人物の系譜にみえる上祖の意富比魁は、オホヒコとよまれ、記紀の大彦命にあたる可能性が高い（平凡社『世界大百科事典』）」。また、[[岸俊男]]（京都大学名誉教授）は次のように解説する。「ヲワケを東国国造の系譜に属する者と考える説と、上祖オホヒコを記紀に阿倍臣や膳臣 (かしわでのおみ) の始祖としてみえる孝元天皇の皇子大彦命とし、あるいは杖刀人は阿倍臣に従属する丈部(はせつかべ) であるとみて、ヲワケを中央豪族の一員と考える説に大きく見解が分かれている（平凡社『世界大百科事典』）」。[[安本美典]]は、『[[本朝皇胤紹運録]]』によると「大彦命」の孫は「豐韓別命」であり、鉄剣銘文の「意富比垝（オホヒコ）」の孫「弖已加利獲居（テヨカリワケ）」と読み方が似ているとする（[https://web.archive.org/web/20070706203736/http://yamatai.cside.com/katudou/kiroku246.htm 倭王武と雄略天皇 稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣銘文]）。また、豐韓別命は[[武渟川別]]の子とされるが、鉄剣銘文では弖已加利獲居は[[多加利足尼]]の子である。</ref>。このことから、四道将軍が創作された神話ではなく、実際に伝承された祖先伝説を元に構成されたことを示している。
[[崇神天皇]]陵に比定されている[[行燈山古墳]]（墳丘全長約242m、後円部直径約158m、後円部高さ約23m）と[[吉備津彦命]]の[[陵墓]]参考地（大吉備津彦命墓）である[[中山茶臼山古墳]]（墳丘全長約120m、後円部直径約80m、後円部高さ約12m）はサイズがほぼ2対1の相似形であることが指摘されている<ref>安本美典 『巨大古墳の主がわかった!』 JICC出版局 1991年。行燈山古墳は[[文久の修陵]]の際に修復が行われており、被葬者については異説もあること、また中山茶臼山古墳は[[吉備の中山]]の自然の地形を利用して築造されていることに注意。</ref>。
== 備考 == * [[前田晴人]]は四道将軍の神話自体は史実とは認めないものの、ここに描かれた「'''四道'''」を[[五畿七道]]成立以前のヤマト王権の地域区分であったとし、四道将軍はその成立を説明するために創作された説話であるとする説を唱えている。 * [[前田晴人]]の2014年時点での見解は<!-- 参考後述書 pp.158 - 171. -->、四道という語は[[6世紀]]の[[国造]]制再編による影響によって導入された中華思想的な概念とする<ref>『ここまでわかった! 「古代」謎の四世紀』 [[新人物文庫]] 2014年 ISBN 978-4-04-600400-0 p.171.</ref>。論拠として、[[5世紀]]の[[倭の五王]]の一人である武（[[雄略天皇]]）が出した[[上表文]]には、「東西南北」ではなく、「東西」を平定したといった内容が記され、東西を基軸とした国家統治を基本として、本来、こちらが弥生期から続く伝統的な観念であり<ref>同『ここまでわかった! 「古代」謎の四世紀』 p.171.</ref>、『[[古事記]]』においても、「西道」（[[吉備国]]）の記述がなく、これにともなって「四道」の語もない<ref>同『ここまでわかった! 「古代」謎の四世紀』 p.161.</ref>ことから、4世紀時点に四道の語はなかったとする。
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[崇神天皇]] * [[大彦命]] * [[武渟川別|武渟川別命]] * [[吉備津彦命]] * [[丹波道主命]] * [[温羅]] * [[倭国大乱]] * [[倭迹迹日百襲姫命]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:しとうしようくん}} [[Category:日本の将軍の称号]] [[Category:日本神話の人物]] [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:日本の名数4|とうしようくん]]

revision.text:
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | Image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

revision.text:
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | Image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Four Commanders of the Four Roads]] to [[Shidō Shōgun]]
revision.text:
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | Image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * [[Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}

=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

revision.text:
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | Image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * [[Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}

{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | Image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.

{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | Image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | Image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
