{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōgen|qq=}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika|qq=}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoku Shrine|qq=}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isasumi Shrine|qq=}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omihakoyama Kofun|qq=}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenta Suka Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōrei|qq=}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Shrine|qq=}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} * [[Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Province|qq=}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tajima Province|qq=}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōrei|qq=}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōgen|qq=}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika|qq=}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Ankoku Shrine|ja|敢國神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoku Shrine|qq=}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isasumi Shrine|qq=}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omihakoyama Kofun|qq=}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōrei|qq=}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Shrine|qq=}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} * [[Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}

He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} (Okayama Prefecture). This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Hikoiwasu no Mikoto]] → [[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Province|qq=}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tajima Province|qq=}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōrei|qq=}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōgen|qq=}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika|qq=}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Aekuni Shrine|ja|敢國神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoku Shrine|qq=}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isasumi Shrine|qq=}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omihakoyama Kofun|qq=}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōrei|qq=}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Shrine|qq=}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} * [[Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|四道将軍|version=103264853|comment=Imported full ja history}}

}}

{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}

They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Province|qq=}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tajima Province|qq=}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōrei|qq=}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōgen|qq=}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika|qq=}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Aekuni Shrine|ja|敢國神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoku Shrine|qq=}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isasumi Shrine|qq=}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omihakoyama Kofun|qq=}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōrei|qq=}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Shrine|qq=}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} * [[Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}

{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, in the 10th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}, the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Province|qq=}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tajima Province|qq=}} under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōrei|qq=}}'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōgen|qq=}} and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika|qq=}}. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}, making him the maternal grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), {{ill|Aekuni Shrine|ja|敢國神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoku Shrine|qq=}} (Mie Prefecture), and {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isasumi Shrine|qq=}} (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the {{ill|Omihakoyama Kofun|ja|御墓山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omihakoyama Kofun|qq=}} in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōrei|qq=}}, subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at {{ill|Kamiya Shrine|ja|神谷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Shrine|qq=}} (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]
{{translated page|ja|四道将軍|version=103264853|comment=Imported full ja history}}

}}

{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}

He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

<!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--> [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:日本の名数4]] [[Category:日本の将軍の称号]]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Characters in Japanese mythology
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]
{{translated page|ja|四道将軍|version=103264853|comment=Imported full ja history}}

}}

He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

<!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--> [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:日本の名数4]] [[Category:日本の将軍の称号]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Shidō shōgun]] [[zh:四道将军]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Figures in Japanese mythology
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]
{{translated page|ja|四道将軍|version=103264853|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}

His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]
