<!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--> [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:日本の名数4]] [[Category:日本の将軍の称号]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Shidō shōgun]] [[zh:四道将军]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Shidō Shōgun
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]

It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]

At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]].

His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).

The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funatsu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funatsu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]

[[sn:Shidō Shōgun]] [[ja:四道将軍]] [[sn:hidō Shōgun]]
<!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--> [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 4]] [[Category:Titles of Japanese Shoguns]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Shidō shōgun]] [[zh:四道将军]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q9076804]] Wikidata name: Shoguns of the Four Provinces [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funazu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funazu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=|1=Inariyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596864}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]

[[ja:四道将軍]]
<!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--> [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 4]] [[Category:Titles of Japanese Shoguns]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Shidō shōgun]] [[zh:四道将军]] {{wikidata link|Q9076804}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[es:Shidō shōgun]][[ja:四道将軍]][[zh:四道将军]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q9076804]] Wikidata name: Shoguns of the Four Provinces [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funazu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funazu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=|1=Inariyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596864}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596864}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]

[[ja:四道将軍]]
<!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--><!--Shidō Shōgun--> [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 4]] [[Category:Titles of Japanese Shoguns]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Shidō shōgun]] [[zh:四道将军]] {{wikidata link|Q9076804}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[es:Shidō shōgun]][[ja:四道将軍]][[zh:四道将军]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q9076804]] Wikidata name: Shoguns of the Four Provinces [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funazu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funazu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596864}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]

The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funazu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funazu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596864}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[es:Shidō shōgun]][[ja:四道将軍]][[zh:四道将军]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q9076804]] Wikidata name: Shoguns of the Four Provinces [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=unsure of the title, whether to keep it in onyomi, turn it into kunyomi, or translate it[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:18, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funazu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funazu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596864}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funazu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funazu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596864}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>

{{Ancient Japanese Generals | Title = Shidō Shōgun (四道将軍) | image = [[File:Rutas shido shogun-han.svg|200px|thumb|The four routes (四道)]] | Historical Context = Generals appointed by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] }}
The '''Shidō Shōgun''' (四道将軍, ancient reading: Yotsu no Michi no Ikusa no Kimi) were generals mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. This title referred to four individuals: [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]], {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}}, [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]], and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="shoki">“Ōbiko no Mikoto was dispatched to the Hokuriku region, Takenunakawawake to the Tōkai region, Kibitsuhiko to the western route, and Tanbanomichinushi to the Tanba region. They were granted the title of general and instructed to raise arms against any who refused their teachings.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref>
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 10th year of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], the four generals were dispatched to the Hokuriku, Tōkai, western, and Tanba regions. At the time, "Tanba" encompassed what later became the [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], and [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]] under the ritsuryō system. They were given the authority to use military force against those who did not accept the imperial decree.<ref name="shoki"/> By the 11th year of Emperor Sujin, they had reportedly subdued regional enemies and returned in triumph.<ref>“The four generals reported the successful pacification of the enemies.” [http://www.j-texts.com/jodai/shoki5.html Nihon Shoki (Asahi Shimbun Edition)]</ref> Emperor Sujin is believed to have lived in the 3rd to 4th centuries.<ref>“Japan’s History, Volume 1: From Myth to History,” Chūō Kōronsha, 1964; Bunko Edition, 2005, ISBN 978-4122045477.</ref><ref>The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] records Emperor Sujin’s death in the year of Tsuchinoe-Tora, corresponding to either 258 or 318 CE.</ref>
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] mentions that Ōbiko was dispatched to the Koshi route (越国), Takenunakawawake to "the twelve eastern routes," and {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwasu no Mikoto|qq=}} to the Tanba region. However, it does not use the term "Shidō Shōgun." It also recounts an earlier episode during [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]'s reign, where [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] and [[Wakatakehiko]] subdued the Kibi region from Harima Province. The term "Shidō Shōgun" is absent in these accounts.
The legends of the Shidō Shōgun are often viewed as mythological narratives that reflect the expansion of early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato]] authority into peripheral regions. The routes of their conquests align closely with the distribution of 4th-century [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]].
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Ōbiko no Mikoto === Ōbiko no Mikoto, the first son of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome]], was the elder brother of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His daughter, [[Mimaki-hime]], became the consort of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], making him the maternal grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]. Ōbiko is enshrined at various shrines, including {{ill|Funazu Shrine|ja|舟津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funazu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture), [[:en:Aekuni Shrine|Aekuni Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture), and [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture). His tomb is believed to be the [[:en:Mihakayama Kofun|Omihakoyama Kofun]] in Mie Prefecture.<ref>Ankoku Shrine's tradition associates Ōbiko with the foundation of the Abe clan (阿倍氏).</ref>
=== Takenunakawawake no Mikoto === Takenunakawawake, son of Ōbiko, is considered an ancestor of the Abe clan (阿倍氏). He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsu Shrine|ja|津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Shrine|qq=}} (Gifu Prefecture) and {{ill|Kenda Suga Shrine|ja|健田須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenta Suka Shrine|qq=}} (Ibaraki Prefecture).
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Ōbiko and Takenunakawawake met at Aizu after pacifying their respective regions. This meeting is believed to have influenced the etymology of Aizu, with the respective conquest routes following the Agano River (Ōbiko) and Kinugawa River (Takenunakawawake).<ref>Masatsune Nakaji, philosopher and historian, proposed this theory.</ref>
=== Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto === Kibitsuhiko, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], subdued the Kibi region, adopting the name Kibitsuhiko. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] (Okayama Prefecture). He is also associated with the legend of Momotarō.
=== Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto === According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tanbanomichinushi was the son of Hikoiwasu no Mikoto and the maternal grandfather of Emperor Keikō. He is enshrined at [[:en:Kandani Shrine|Kamiya Shrine]] (Kyoto Prefecture).
== Archaeological Evidence ==
The {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596864}} in Saitama Prefecture yielded an inscribed iron sword mentioning Ōbiko as a possible ancestor. This corroborates the historical existence of the Shidō Shōgun.<ref>Katsuyasu Kawaguchi, Tokyo Metropolitan University; Toshio Kishi, Kyoto University.</ref>
== Notes == * Historian Haruto Maeda views the Shidō Shōgun as a retrospective narrative created to explain Yamato's regional divisions.<ref>“Understanding Ancient Japan,” Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 2014.</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Prince Ōhiko]] * {{ill|Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto|lt=Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|ja|武渟川別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenunakawawake no Mikoto|qq=}} * [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] * {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanbanomichinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} * [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]
{{translated page|ja|四道将軍|version=103264853|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]]
[[Category:Japanese mythology]]
}}
